nativeRange.habitat: Deep coastal drop-offs and dead-reef areas in somewhat turbid water, typically at depths of 40-54 m (broader range 20-100 m).

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs

diet.notes: Active planktivore in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp and enriched frozen brine shrimp, and over time may take high-quality flake foods. Multiple small feedings per day are recommended, and an attached refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods is widely advised to keep this species in long-term condition.

avoidWith: other Pseudanthias males in cramped quarters
very boisterous tankmates that outcompete it for food

imageCandidates.notes: John E. Randall (SAIAB via GBIF), 600x400. Live in-situ male: vibrant pink-red body, yellow-tipped lyretail, blue-edged fins, with the diagnostic reddish dorsal spot visible on the upper back. Sharp, well-framed, whole animal in view.
John E. Randall (SAIAB via GBIF), 600x400. Second live in-situ male in rose-pink with bright yellow dorsal and caudal fins against reef; sharp and colourful. Alternate angle of the same species.

summary: The Bimaculatus Anthias is a deep-water Indo-Pacific reef fish that does best in a large aquarium with several daily feedings, open swimming space, and a group of at least three — ideally one male kept with several females.
careNotes: The Bimaculatus Anthias is a planktivore from deeper reef slopes — typically 40 to 54 metres down — and is generally considered an intermediate-level species. Cool, well-oxygenated water, ample open swimming space, and several small feedings per day are commonly advised. Like other members of Pseudanthias, it is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals begin life as female and a dominant individual can transition to male. This species naturally forms harems consisting of one dominant male with several females; if the male is lost, the largest female has been reported to transition into the male role. It is generally best kept as a group of one male with three to five females rather than as a solitary specimen, and males tend to be aggressive toward other males in the same tank. Anthias are widely considered strong jumpers, and a tightly sealed lid is generally recommended.
breedingNotes: The Bimaculatus Anthias is not routinely bred in home aquaria. It is a protogynous hermaphrodite and broadcast spawner, releasing eggs and sperm freely into the water column. Closely related species such as Pseudanthias pleurotaenia have been reported to spawn alongside this species and occasionally produce hybrids in the wild.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, distribution, depth/temperature range, and max length. FishBase max length 14.0 cm TL is larger than LiveAquaria's stated ~13 cm; size range broadened to 10-14 cm. FishBase preferred temperature 25.6-28.4 C is warmer than LiveAquaria's 22.2-25.6 C; final range 23-27 C averages the two.
